title: Apple's AI Surrender Could Be the Biggest Strategic Blunder of the Decade

source.original: A Return to Code – Naval Ravikant on Vibe Coding, Personal App Stores, and the End of Apple's Dominance

body_html:
Naval Ravikant draws a direct line from Apple's failure to build competitive AI to a structural erosion of its platform monopoly. His argument: as user interactions shift from tapping through app interfaces to conversing with AI agents, the value of Apple's meticulously designed OS and curated App Store diminishes. If all you're doing is telling an agent to call you an Uber or track your workout, you don't need Apple's human interface guidelines—you need a screen, a battery, and connectivity. Android provides all three.

The comparison to Microsoft's miss on mobile is deliberate and pointed. Microsoft clung to Windows and enterprise customers while Apple built a consumer-first, touchscreen-native OS from the ground up. The result was a generational shift in market dominance. Ravikant sees Apple making the same category of mistake—clinging to its hardware-plus-ecosystem model while the interaction paradigm moves to conversational AI, a layer Apple doesn't control.

Making matters worse, Apple is now dependent on Google's Gemini for AI capabilities. This is a company that built its brand on vertical integration and owning the full stack. Outsourcing its AI brain to a competitor means Apple's differentiation collapses to hardware quality—which supports Samsung-like margins, not Apple-like margins. The market cap implications are significant.

Ravikant demonstrates this concretely with his personal app store. He describes an app to Claude, it compiles and ships to his iPhone in minutes, and he installs it with one tap. The entire creative and distribution loop bypasses the App Store. Apple still gates wide distribution, but the writing is on the wall: when agents can generate custom interfaces on the fly, the static app paradigm looks increasingly antiquated.

None of this means Apple disappears. Like Microsoft, it can remain enormously valuable for decades. But Ravikant believes Apple's future growth is capped unless it reverses course on AI—and that the gap between what Apple is worth and what it could have been worth will only widen as agentic computing matures.
